notes: Expletive there is treated as a pronoun.
Non-existential there is treated as an intransitive preposition; expletive there is handled separately.
Non-expletive there is deferred to later cleanup.
Contracted n't is treated as a negative morpheme rather than a separate word.
Articles are determinatives.
Foreign-language une is treated as an article-like determinative when UD tags it as DET before an X head.
Mat in the quoted foreign string Mat Fereder is not treated as a determinative.
Quantificational determinatives keep their category in headed and headless uses.
Marker-like uses of both either and neither remain determinatives even when UD tags them as CCONJ.
Marker-like uses of both either and neither remain determinatives even when UD tags them as ADV.
Half is treated as a noun
column15: including uses UD tags as DET or ADJ.

notes: Word-form cardinal factors in magnitude constructions are determinatives functioning as modifiers.
Word-form cardinals in determiner function are determinatives.
Word-form cardinals outside simple determiner position are treated as fused-head determinatives unless a narrower rule applies.
PRON one followed by of is treated as a cardinal fused-head determinative in partitives.
Reciprocal one in one another remains a pronoun.
Negative one in no one remains a pronoun.
Generic one remains a pronoun rather than a numerative.
Bare PRON/PRP one is treated as a generic pronoun when UD omits PronType=Prs.
Headed demonstratives are determinatives.
Yonder in determiner use is treated as a demonstrative determinative.
Relative that is a subordinator when UD omits PronType=Rel but enhanced dependencies still mark a ref relation.
Relative that is a subordinator
Relative that in acl:relcl structure is a subordinator even when UD omits or mangles PronType.
Headless demonstratives are determinatives once relative that has been handled by higher-priority rules.
Headed what and which are interrogative determinatives.
Relative which in acl:relcl structure is treated as a fused-head determinative when UD omits PronType.
Headless or interrogative what and which are treated as determinatives in the MVP.
And is a clear coordinator.
But is a clear coordinator.
Or and nor are clear coordinators.
Plus is treated as a coordinator when UD tags it as CCONJ.
Slash is treated as a coordinator when UD uses it as a cc marker.
For marking an infinitival clause is treated as a subordinator.
UD SCONJ so in purpose or result use is treated as a preposition taking a clausal complement.
UD SCONJ that is treated as a subordinator rather than a preposition.
Yonder outside determiner use is treated as an intransitive preposition.
Locative where is treated as an intransitive preposition rather than a pronoun.
Temporal when is treated as an intransitive preposition rather than a pronoun.
UD SCONJ whether is treated as a subordinator rather than a preposition.
UD SCONJ if is treated as a subordinator in clear complement-clause uses.
UD SCONJ if is treated as a subordinator in coordinated complement-clause uses.
UD SCONJ items other than the explicit subordinator class are treated as prepositions taking clausal complements.
ADV-tagged items in the intransitive-preposition set are treated as prepositions rather than adverbs.
Infinitival to is treated as a highly defective auxiliary verb.
UD sometimes tags dependent possessives as DET; they remain pronouns in the target analysis.
Dependent possessive her remains pronoun in determiner function.
Possessive pronouns remain pronouns.
Ordinals are adjectives in the target analysis.
Fractionals are nouns in the target analysis even when UD assigns a different category.
Core personal pronouns plus archaic or dialectal variants.
Reflexive pronouns.
Tokenized self in self-compounds is treated as a nounal compound initial rather than a pronoun.
Canonical wh-pronouns.
UD coordinator-like yet is treated as an adversative coordinator.
UD coordinator-like as well as is treated as an additive coordinator.
Along with introducing a clause is treated as a preposition with a clausal complement.
Along with introducing an NP is treated as a complex preposition.
UD coordinator-like rather than is treated as a coordinator in alternation/preference uses.
Clause-marking rather than is treated as a preposition with a clausal complement.
Et in et al. is treated as an additive coordinator.
Indefinite items like someone and nothing are treated as determinatives rather than pronouns.
Other is treated as an adjective rather than a determinative.
Such is treated as an adjective rather than a determinative.
Whichever is treated as a determinative across headed and fused-head uses.
Whatever is treated as a determinative across headed and fused-head uses.
Dialectal them in determiner function is treated as a determinative.
DET-tagged quite is treated as an adverb; predeterminer is a function
Wtf is treated as an orthographic reduction of headless interrogative what the fuck.
Delexicalized segmented what-ever can be recovered as determinative whatever.
Delexicalized relative who and whom can be recovered from WP length plus ref dependency.
Delexicalized relative that can be recovered from WDT length plus ref dependency.
Delexicalized relative which can be recovered from WDT length plus ref dependency.
Delexicalized possessive pronouns can be recovered structurally from Poss=Yes features.
Delexicalized reflexive pronouns can be recovered structurally from Reflex=Yes features.
Delexicalized expletive pronouns can be recovered structurally from expl dependency.
Delexicalized articles can be recovered structurally from PronType=Art.
Delexicalized headed demonstratives can be recovered structurally from PronType=Dem on DET.
Delexicalized headless demonstratives can be recovered structurally from PronType=Dem on PRON.
Delexicalized determinatives with quantificational PronType features can be recovered structurally.
Delexicalized headed wh-determinatives can be recovered structurally.
Delexicalized personal pronouns can be recovered structurally from PronType=Prs when no lexical rule applies.
Delexicalized indefinite determinatives can be recovered structurally from PronType features.
Catch-all PRON fallback for the pilot.
Catch-all DET fallback for the pilot.
